mnemonics: CPU Time = I * CPI * T (Think: 'I Can't Process Instructions Today' for the three factors: Instructions, Cycles Per Instruction, and Cycle Time)
For better performance, lower the right side: Fewer Instructions, fewer Cycles per Instruction, or a shorter Cycle Time.
keyPoints: CPU Time = Instruction Count * Cycles Per Instruction * Clock Cycle Time
Performance is inversely proportional to execution time; improving performance means decreasing execution time.
The equation highlights the three fundamental levers for improving processor performance: algorithm/compiler efficiency (I), instruction set complexity (CPI), and hardware technology/speed (T).
commonMistakes: Confusing CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) with IPC (Instructions Per Cycle), which is its inverse (IPC = 1/CPI).
Forgetting that the equation is for CPU time, not clock speed; a higher clock speed (lower T) is only one part of the overall performance picture.
Assuming that a lower CPI is always better without considering the trade-off; a more complex instruction (higher CPI) might actually reduce the total Instruction Count (I).
